title: Executive Order 12264—Export of Banned or Significantly Restricted Substances

flags.justification: Sec. 1-301 explicitly states the order's intent 'to rely primarily on the notification procedures... and to resort to the imposition of export controls only in a very few instances' — a genuinely self-limiting design constraining discretion toward the least restrictive available tool, implemented through a six-agency task force.
Sec. 1-303 provides a clear escalation mechanism: if State and Commerce cannot agree, the matter is referred to the President.
Extremely comprehensive substance-category definitions (foods, drugs, devices, pesticides, chemicals) all coherently serve one purpose: banned-substance export policy.

Extensive, precise cross-references to dozens of specific statutory provisions define 'banned or significantly restricted substance,' and export-control decision criteria (Sec. 1-305) are concrete and enumerated.
No incentive structure rewarding behavior contrary to the order's purpose is identifiable.
A standing regulatory framework implementing a permanent statute (the Export Administration Act), evergreen by nature.
This creates extensive new notification, reporting, and task-force review requirements beyond bare statutory minimums; no existing oversight is removed.

Sec. 1-6 requires an independent annual evaluation report on the order's own effectiveness (by CEQ, State, and Commerce), and Sec. 1-306 requires the State Department to consider the affected foreign government's own input before any export license is recommended — genuine, multiple external and self-evaluative checks.

Establishes a significant, novel international public-health notification regime, predecessor to later chemical and hazardous-substance export notification norms — a meaningful institutional innovation, though deliberately bounded and export-control-averse by design.
The six-agency interagency task force (Sec. 1-304), explicitly tasked to 'endeavor to reach consensus,' is a carefully coordinated design.

Sec. 1-701's trade-secret protection is a protective limitation on information-sharing, not a coverage exemption; the substantive scope of covered substances is comprehensively defined, not carved out from.

notes: Validation batch (positive). Structural weight: 1/22 = 4.5%. Issued five days before the end of the Carter administration; one of the most exemplary orders coded in this project — explicit self-restraint favoring notification over export controls, a built-in self-evaluation requirement, and extensive multi-agency consensus review. Fourth in a run of well-designed late-Carter orders alongside EO 12088, EO 12139, and EO 12217.
